title: Plastic waste generation
subtitle: This measures total plastic waste generation prior to management and therefore does not represent the quantity of plastic at risk of polluting waterways, rivers and the ocean environment.
caption:
Plastic waste generation, 2010

This measures total plastic waste generation prior to management and therefore does not represent the quantity of plastic at risk of polluting waterways, rivers and the ocean environment.

The choropleth map displays the total mass of plastic waste generated by country in 2010, utilizing a continuous color scale ranging from pale beige for zero tonnes to deep maroon for values exceeding 10 million tonnes. The visualization reveals a highly concentrated distribution of waste generation, with the highest volumes clustered in a small number of large economies across North America, South America, and East Asia. The United States and China appear in the darkest shade, identifying them as the top generators with totals surpassing the 10 million tonne threshold. Brazil also falls within this maximum category, distinguished by its deep red coloring in South America. Moving down the scale, countries such as Russia, India, and Germany occupy the intermediate bands, with Russia and India generating between 5 million and 7.5 million tonnes, while Germany sits in the 2.5 million to 5 million tonne range. Canada, Mexico, and Australia display lighter orange hues, indicating generation levels between 1 million and 2.5 million tonnes. Large portions of the African continent, including nations in the central and western regions, along with Greenland, are marked with diagonal hatching to signify that no data is available for these areas. The remaining landmass, including most of South America outside Brazil and parts of Southeast Asia, is shaded in pale beige or light orange, representing generation figures below 1 million tonnes or just slightly above. The data shows that global plastic waste generation in 2010 was heavily skewed toward a few specific industrialized and populous nations, while vast geographic areas contributed minimal recorded volumes or lacked reporting entirely.
